Japan.status: DRAW IS ENOUGH — a draw today guarantees top-2 finish regardless of NED-TUN result

SWE.NOTE: SWE averaged 3 offsides in BOTH matches — dominant win AND heavy loss. Extremely consistent. SWE generated 8 SOT even in a 5-1 defeat vs NED.

context_flags: JPN_CONSERVATIVE: Japan only needs a draw — may play more cautiously in 2H if leading or level
SWE_MUST_WIN: Sweden maximum attacking intent, especially in 2H
SWE_OFFSIDE_CONSISTENCY: 3 offsides in BOTH WC2026 matches regardless of game state — structural pattern
SWE_HIGH_SOT: 7 SOT vs TUN, 8 SOT vs NED (even in loss) — Sweden generates shots regardless
BLOWOUT_RULE10: If JPN leads comfortably, SWE desperation increases offside risk
JPN_LOW_SOT: Japan averaged only 4 SOT/game in WC2026 vs Sweden's 7.5

questions.text: Will Sweden be caught offside 2 or more times?
questions.reasoning: Sweden caught offside exactly 3 times in GD1 (dominant 5-1 win vs TUN) and 3 times in GD2 (5-1 loss to NED). Entirely consistent regardless of game state. Today SWE must-win → more aggressive forward runs → offside risk increases further. Japan plays compact. First-pass Poisson λ=3.0 gives P(≥2)=0.80. Submit near-raw per WINNING TRAIL.

questions.text: In the second half, will Japan have more shots on target than Sweden?
questions.reasoning: Sweden WC2026 SOT: 7 (vs TUN), 8 (vs NED) — 7.5 avg. Japan: 3 (vs NED), 5 (vs TUN) — 4.0 avg. Sweden out-shot NED in SOT (8-7) even while losing 5-1. Sweden is a high-volume shooting team regardless of scoreline. In 2H, SWE's must-win urgency means even more attacking. For Japan to out-SOT Sweden in 2H they'd need to significantly over-perform history. Skellam P(JPN>SWE in 2H) ≈ 0.20.

questions.text: Will Sweden score more goals than Japan in the second half?
questions.reasoning: No direct 2H result market. SWE must-win = more 2H attacking. But JPN draw-is-enough = can sit back. SWE has higher goal rate expected. Rough estimate: P(SWE>JPN in 2H) ≈ 0.28. Medium confidence — no direct market anchor.

questions.text: Will a penalty kick be awarded OR a red card be shown?
questions.reasoning: Market: Penalty 25.1%, Red card 10.4%. P(OR) = 1 - (0.749 × 0.896) = 0.33. This question family has been reliable: JPN-TUN +2.44, SWE-TUN +11.68. Crowd drifts to 0.40-0.45 on this type. Trust the compound market at 0.33.
Smarkets FTR: Japan 46.58%. Direct tier-1 market. No reason to deviate.

questions.text: Will the match have 2 or fewer total goals?
questions.reasoning: Market OU2.5 Under = 49.56%. BTTS = 57%. Coin flip. No structural edge. Submit at market.

questions.text: Will Sweden have 5 or more corner kicks?
questions.reasoning: SWE corners in WC2026: 4 (vs TUN), 5 (vs NED). Average 4.5. Market SWE OU4.5 Over = 40.4%. SWE must-win = more attacking = potentially more corners. Trust market at 0.40.

questions.text: Will Viktor Gyökeres score a goal (excluding own goals)?
questions.reasoning: Smarkets anytime goalscorer: 29.6%. Gyökeres scored in GD1 (vs TUN at 59'), did not score in GD2 (vs NED). SWE must-win increases his role but RULE15 applies mildly (SWE 25.5% underdog). Submit near market.

strongest_picks: Q1: Sweden 2+ offsides = 0.78 (HIGHEST CONFIDENCE — λ=3.0 consistent across both WC matches, must-win context reinforces)
Q2: Japan more 2H SOT = 0.20 (STRONG NO — ESPN data very clear, SWE 7.5 avg vs JPN 4.0 avg SOT)
Q4: Penalty or RC = 0.33 (market-compound, reliable NO family)

Q1_SWE_2plus_offsides.note: Sweden's offside consistency (3 offsides in BOTH GD1 win and GD2 loss) held a third time. The single highest-conviction pick of the day paid off biggest — direct confirmation of the structural λ=3.0 pattern.

Q2_JPN_more_2H_SOT.note: Biggest single win of the match, and arguably the cleanest ESPN-data read all tournament — Sweden's 7.5 avg SOT vs Japan's 4.0 avg SOT held up exactly as modeled, while the crowd treated it as a 50/50.

Q7_SWE_5plus_corners.note: Small loss — Sweden's must-win urgency generated slightly more corners than our market-anchored 0.40 expected.

key_confirmations: Q1 + Q2 alone = +45.56 RBP, both from pure ESPN-boxscore Poisson/Skellam reads submitted near-raw with no narrative shading — the cleanest WINNING TRAIL execution of the day.
Q2 in particular is now a template case: when a team's average stat (SOT) is roughly double the opponent's across multiple matches, trust the model fully even when the crowd insists on near-50/50.
